entry_requirements.asvab_line_score: CL (Clerical) composite ≥ 105, per two independent secondary sources (operationmilitarykids.org, battalionduty.com), consistent with the Army:CL composite listed for this cluster in data/interest-taxonomy.json. UNVERIFIED at primary-source level — the official goarmy.com 27D page did not return score data via this session's web-search-based retrieval; confirm the current cutoff with a recruiter.
entry_requirements.education: High school diploma or GED (Army general accession standard; no 27D-specific deviation found).
entry_requirements.age_range: 17 (with parental consent) to 42, per the Army's 2026 policy ceiling — data/branch-profiles.json. No 27D-specific age restriction found.
entry_requirements.physical: Standard Army accession medical and fitness standards (Army Fitness Test/AFT); no elevated physical/vision standard specific to 27D was found.
entry_requirements.security_clearance: Secret security clearance required. The clearance investigation runs in parallel with enlistment processing and typically takes 2–6 months to complete (battalionduty.com, operationmilitarykids.org, cross-corroborated this session).

training_pipeline.location: One of four Army training centers (Fort Jackson SC, Fort Moore GA, Fort Sill OK, or Fort Leonard Wood MO)
training_pipeline.description: Standard Army basic training common to all MOSs. (data/branch-profiles.json)

training_pipeline.stage: Advanced Individual Training (AIT) — Paralegal Specialist Course
training_pipeline.location: Fort Gregg-Adams, VA (formerly Fort Lee); one secondary source cited Fort Jackson, SC — minor cross-source location variance, not resolved to primary confidence this session.
training_pipeline.description: Teaches the military justice system, court-martial and administrative-separation procedures, legal-assistance intake, and legal-document preparation — the Army teaches the military legal system from the ground up rather than assuming prior legal knowledge. Length reported consistently at ~10 weeks across secondary sources; not independently confirmed against a primary Army source this session.

pay_and_compensation.methodology_note: Computed, not authoritative: (E-1, <2 YOS basic pay $2,407.20/mo + enlisted BAS $476.95/mo) x 12 = $34,609.80/yr. BAH is intentionally EXCLUDED because it is duty-station-specific and cannot be reduced to one national number (pay-tables.json bah.national_number_warning) — actual total cash compensation is materially higher once a real BAH figure for the member's duty station is added. Excludes the DoD TSP contribution (up to 5% of base pay) and TRICARE's non-cash value.

bonuses.conditions: No current, verifiable 27D-specific enlistment or reenlistment bonus figure was found this session (no dedicated bonus search was run for 27D within the ~10-call cluster budget). Clerical/administrative-legal enlisted specialties are not typically shortage MOSs that draw large bonuses, by the same pattern documented for the culinary cluster in data/specialties-culinary.json. Bonuses are set quarterly per Army manning needs — verify current 27D bonus eligibility and amount directly with a recruiter or the HRC Bonus Chart before relying on any number.
bonuses.source: Not independently searched this session; general bonus-structure pattern from data/specialties-culinary.json

career_progression.typical_promotion_timeline: E-1→E-4 in roughly 2-3 years is the Army-wide norm; E-5 (Sergeant) board eligibility typically follows at ~4-6 years of service. No 27D-specific timeline deviation was found — treat as the general Army enlisted pattern.
reenlistment_decision_points: End of initial term of service (commonly a 3- or 4-year first contract)
Selection for the Paralegal NCO Course / advanced legal-specialist training at each promotion gate
The 20-year retention decision (pension cliff — see retirement block)
advanced_schools_or_certs: Advanced paralegal coursework at The Judge Advocate General's Legal Center and School (TJAGLCS), Charlottesville, VA — name/curriculum not independently confirmed to primary-source level this session
Basic/Advanced NCO courses at each promotion gate (standard Army NCOES, not 27D-specific)
career_progression.officer_conversion_path: No direct ASVAB-to-JAG pathway exists (see the binding credential-gap note in this record's civilian_crosswalk and the separate JAG officer records in this file). An enlisted 27D who wants to become a Judge Advocate must separately earn a bachelor's degree, gain admission to and complete a JD at an ABA-accredited law school, and pass a state bar exam — then apply through a JAG accession program (e.g., Direct Appointment, or a service's Funded Legal Education Program where enlisted members are eligible, as confirmed for the Air Force this session). Enlisted paralegal experience may strengthen such an application but does not substitute for any of these steps. Army-specific enlisted-to-FLEP eligibility was not independently confirmed this session.

retirement.twenty_year_cliff_note: The pension is all-or-nothing at 20 years — no partial/prorated defined-benefit pension for separating early. Before 20 years, a paralegal specialist keeps every dollar of their own TSP contributions and earnings (always vested), plus DoD's automatic 1%-of-pay TSP contribution and match (vested after 2 years) — all portable into a civilian 401(k)/IRA. The defined-benefit pension itself is 'entirely forfeited' below 20 years, per DoD's own language. Applies identically regardless of specialty.
retirement.continuation_pay_note: One-time cash payout available between roughly year 7 and year 12 of service; Army's published active-component rate is 2.5x monthly basic pay for a 4-year additional service obligation. No 27D-specific continuation-pay rate was identified — treat the general BRS figure as baseline.
retirement.guard_reserve_points_note: Guard/Reserve retirement is NOT interchangeable with active-duty retirement. Points system (1 point/drill, 1 point/AD day, up to 15 membership points/year) toward a 20-'good year' qualifying threshold, non-regular pension generally starting at age 60 (reducible, not below 50, for qualifying post-2008 mobilized duty). Applies identically to Guard/Reserve 27D billets.
retirement.lump_sum_option_note: At 20+ years, a retiring member may elect 25% or 50% of the discounted present value of future retired pay as an immediate lump sum, with correspondingly reduced monthly retired pay until Full Social Security Retirement Age (~67). Fully taxable in the year received.

transition.skillbridge_notes: DoD-wide SkillBridge eligibility (up to 180 days of permissive duty in the final pre-separation window) is not specialty-restricted — subject to the same real gate as everyone else: unit commander approval, which is discretionary based on unit staffing/operational needs (data/transition.json). Whether legal-industry SkillBridge placements (law firms, legal-staffing agencies) are commonly available in a given service member's location was not independently verified this session.
transition.tap_program_summary: Mandatory DoD-wide Transition Assistance Program: pre-separation counseling, VA Benefits I course, DOL employment-fundamentals workshop, and an optional 2-day track (Higher Education, Career/Technical Training, or Entrepreneurship), ending in a Capstone certifying Career Readiness Standards are met. Must begin no later than 365 days before separation. Applies identically regardless of specialty.
transition.dd214_significance: The DD-214's 'character of service' entry gates eligibility for nearly every benefit below — VA disability, VA healthcare, GI Bill, VA home loan, and most state veteran benefits. No 27D-specific significance beyond the general rule.
va_disability_common_claims: GUESS/INFERENCE, not sourced from a specialty-linked VA claims dataset this session (data/transition.json's specialty_linked_common_claims list covers combat arms, aviation, infantry, and PACT Act theater deployment, but does NOT include a legal/paralegal entry). Reasoned by analogy to the desk-bound, high-caseload nature of the job rather than pulled from a VA claims-frequency source:
Musculoskeletal/repetitive-strain conditions (neck, back, carpal tunnel) from years of prolonged computer/data-entry work preparing legal documents
Vision strain from sustained close document review

Mental-health conditions (secondary traumatic stress, anxiety) plausibly elevated by repeated exposure to case files involving sexual assault, death, and serious misconduct in the course of processing courts-martial and administrative-separation actions — this is a reasoned inference, not a sourced claims-frequency figure
To verify: consult VA's published claims-by-occupation data (if published) or a VSO familiar with legal/administrative ratings before treating this list as authoritative.
transition.vr_e_eligibility_note: Requires a 20%+ service-connected disability rating creating an employment handicap, filed within a 12-year window from separation or first rating. Funds up to 48 months of training/education plus a monthly subsistence allowance (FY2026 max ~$3,439.23/mo per data/transition.json), drawn from a separate entitlement bucket than the Post-9/11 GI Bill. No 27D-specific eligibility variance.

relevant_certifications: NALA Certified Paralegal (CP) — independent exam plus education/experience prerequisites; not conferred automatically
NFPA PACE-based Registered Paralegal (RP) credential — independent exam; not conferred automatically
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: BINDING: a military paralegal specialist is NOT automatically a certified civilian paralegal. NALA's Certified Paralegal (CP) and NFPA's PACE-based Registered Paralegal credentials are conferred by independent nonprofit certifying bodies, not by DoD, and each requires meeting that body's own education prerequisite (commonly an ABA-approved paralegal-studies program, or a bachelor's degree plus documented paralegal work experience) plus passing a proctored exam — military MOS training and OJT hours do not automatically satisfy this. This gap is reasoned by direct analogy to the credential-gap pattern established across every other cluster in this project (data/transition.json's credential_gap block covering medics/EMT-P, MPs/POST, aircraft mechanics/A&P, etc.) and to the general licensing-body structure of NALA/NFPA; it was NOT independently re-confirmed against nala.org or paralegals.org within this session's tool budget — verify current certification prerequisites directly with NALA (nala.org) or NFPA (paralegals.org) before publishing as settled fact. Separately and distinctly: a military paralegal is also not a lawyer and cannot practice law — see the JAG officer records in this file for the actual attorney credential path, which requires an entirely separate JD + bar admission, not an upgrade from this enlisted track.
civilian_crosswalk.source: BLS OEWS Paralegals and Legal Assistants (23-2011), May 2024 reference period — https://www.bls.gov/oes/2023/may/oes232011.htm (retrieved via web search synthesis this session; only the median figure was returned, not the full 10th/90th percentile table — the range above is therefore a single median data point duplicated across both array positions, not a true low/high spread; treat as UNVERIFIED range pending a direct fetch of the BLS OEWS page).

veteran_benefits.gi_bill_tier_note: Post-9/11 GI Bill (Ch. 33) benefit tier scales with cumulative qualifying active-duty days: 100% at 36+ months (or Purple Heart / service-connected-disability discharge after 30+ days), down through 90%, 80%, 70%, 60%, to a 50% floor at 90 days-5 months. No tier below 50%. Only qualifying Title 10 active-duty days count. Applies identically regardless of specialty.
veteran_benefits.gi_bill_transferability_note: Transferable to a spouse and/or dependent children (up to 36 months combined) after 6 years of service, in exchange for a 4-year additional service-obligation commitment. Must be requested via milConnect while still serving; cannot be initiated after separation. Dependent children must wait until the member has 10+ years of service to start using it.
veteran_benefits.va_home_loan_note: VA-backed home loans generally require no down payment and no monthly PMI for veterans with full entitlement, subject to lender underwriting and a funding fee (1.25%-3.3% of loan amount depending on down payment and first/subsequent use, waived for veterans drawing service-connected disability compensation).
veteran_benefits.va_healthcare_priority_group_note: VA assigns one of 8 priority groups at enrollment based on service-connected disability rating, combat/toxic-exposure service history, income, and other VA-benefit receipt — not by specialty. A paralegal specialist's group assignment depends entirely on their individual disability rating and income profile.
veteran_benefits.state_benefit_variance_note: State veteran benefits (property-tax exemptions, income-tax treatment, tuition waivers, etc.) are set by individual state legislatures and are NOT a single national figure (data/veteran-benefits.json). Verify against the veteran's specific state of legal residence.

education_benefits.gi_bill_summary: Post-9/11 GI Bill pays full tuition/fees at public schools (in-state rate applies to all users) or up to $29,920.95/yr (2025-2026 AY) at private/foreign schools, plus a housing allowance keyed to the school's zip code and a $1,000/yr book stipend — scaled by the eligibility tier above. (data/education-benefits.json)
education_benefits.tuition_assistance: DoD-wide Tuition Assistance: $250/semester-hour, capped at $4,500/fiscal year, while still serving. Applies the same across all six branches per data/education-benefits.json.
education_benefits.credentialing_assistance: Army Credentialing Assistance (CA)/Army COOL: reduced to a $2,000/fiscal-year cap in 2024, lifetime limit reported at 3 credentials per 10 years of service. UNVERIFIED at primary-source level — cool.osd.mil could not be independently fetched this session. (data/education-benefits.json)
education_benefits.coop_or_apprenticeship_notes: Army COOL likely lists NALA CP and/or NFPA-related credentials against 27D, consistent with how COOL maps other MOSs to civilian certifications — this was NOT independently confirmed against cool.osd.mil this session (consistent with education-benefits.json's own note that primary cool.osd.mil fetches in this project have repeatedly failed with certificate errors). Check cool.osd.mil/army/moc/index.html?moc=27d for the current gap-analysis document before publishing.

deployment_and_lifestyle.typical_tempo: Deploys with the supporting legal office/brigade to provide operational-law and military-justice support; tempo generally follows the supported command's operational tempo rather than a specialty-specific rotation. No sourced deployment-frequency figure specific to 27D was found this session.
deployment_and_lifestyle.family_considerations: Garrison legal-office assignments are relatively family-stable; deployments follow the supported unit's schedule, which varies widely by unit type.

specialties.code: JA — Judge Advocate General's Corps (Army basic branch/functional area designator for commissioned attorneys, not an MOS)

entry_requirements.asvab_line_score_note: Explicitly null, not an omission: the JAG Corps is a DIRECT COMMISSION path for licensed attorneys, not an enlistment path. There is no ASVAB line-score gate for this track at all; eligibility instead runs entirely on academic credential (JD) and professional licensure (bar admission). A 16-24-year-old reading this should understand: you cannot enlist your way into being a JAG officer. You go to college, then law school, then take the bar exam, and only then apply for a direct commission as an attorney.
entry_requirements.education: A JD from an ABA-accredited law school, held at the time of entry onto active duty. The ABA-accreditation requirement on the law school may be waived where the applicant holds an LL.M from an ABA-accredited school. (jagcnet.army.mil/GoArmyJAG Eligibility page, via ucmj.us summary this session)
entry_requirements.age_range: Under 42 at time of entry into the JAG Corps; years of prior commissioned military service increase the age ceiling; waivers for those exceeding the limit are considered in meritorious cases. (ucmj.us, cross-referenced against jagcnet.army.mil's Eligibility page listed in this session's search results)
entry_requirements.physical: Standard Army officer-commissioning medical and fitness standards apply — no elevated JAG-specific physical standard was found. Not independently confirmed to primary-source level this session.
entry_requirements.security_clearance: Not independently confirmed to a specific level this session. Reasoned inference: given exposure to classified operational-law and investigative matters, a Secret clearance at minimum (with Top Secret for some assignments) is plausible but was NOT sourced this session — verify before publishing.
entry_requirements.other: BINDING REQUIREMENT: admission to the bar of the highest court of any U.S. State, Commonwealth, Territory, or the District of Columbia is required at the time of entry onto active duty — a JD alone does NOT qualify a candidate. Passing the bar exam and being admitted to practice is a separate, additional gate beyond completing law school. (jagcnet.army.mil, via ucmj.us this session)

training_pipeline.description: Basic military familiarization for newly commissioned officers with no prior military background (physical training, land navigation, weapons familiarization, military customs/courtesies). Length reported as 6 weeks by garrettham.com this session; a note in the same search suggests GoArmyJAG's FAQ may now list an 8-week DCC — the exact current length was NOT resolved to primary-source confidence and should be confirmed at jagcnet.army.mil/GoArmyJAG/FAQ before publishing.

training_pipeline.location: The Judge Advocate General's Legal Center and School (TJAGLCS), Charlottesville, VA
training_pipeline.description: Substantive legal training in military criminal law, tort law, government contract and fiscal law, legal assistance, administrative law, and international/operational law, plus JAG Corps organization and mission. Traditionally reported as 10.5 weeks (rounded to 11 here); this session's search also surfaced a report that JAOBC may have lengthened to 12 weeks — the exact current length was NOT resolved to primary-source confidence. Confirm at tjaglcs.army.mil/courses before publishing.

pay_and_compensation.paygrade_entry: O-2 (First Lieutenant) — typical entry grade for a new JD graduate with no prior commissioned service. This specific accession-grade figure is a moderate-confidence structural fact widely reported in JAG recruiting/informational material but was NOT independently re-confirmed against a primary jagcnet.army.mil page this session; a higher initial grade (e.g., O-3/Captain) is possible with additional professional legal experience or prior military service. Verify before publishing.
pay_and_compensation.methodology_note: Computed, not authoritative: (O-2, <2 YOS basic pay $4,782.00/mo + officer BAS $328.48/mo) x 12 = $61,325.76/yr. BAH is intentionally EXCLUDED because it is duty-station-specific and cannot be reduced to one national number (pay-tables.json bah.national_number_warning). Excludes the DoD TSP contribution and TRICARE's non-cash value. Also excludes any Judge Advocate Continuation Pay or Student Loan Repayment Program payout — see the bonuses block below.

bonuses.conditions: The Army JAG Corps advertises 'financial incentives' for accession (per an American Bar Association article title surfaced this session: 'Army JAG Careers Provide Financial Incentives for Those Interested in Foregoing the Traditional Path'), but this session did NOT retrieve specific current dollar amounts for the Army's program — the confirmed dollar figures below are Air Force-specific (see the Air Force JAG officer record in this file) and should NOT be assumed to apply identically to the Army. Per this cluster's binding constraint: JAG accession/loan-repayment bonuses vary by branch and change over time — mark UNVERIFIED rather than quoting a stale or cross-branch number. Verify current Army JAG financial-incentive amounts directly at jagcnet.army.mil/GoArmyJAG/Benefits.
bonuses.source: https://www.jagcnet.army.mil/GoArmyJAG/Benefits ; https://www.americanbar.org/groups/young_lawyers/resources/after-the-bar/personal-financial/army-jag-careers-financial-incentives-foregoing-traditional-path/ (title/summary only, not independently fetched for dollar figures this session)

career_progression.typical_promotion_timeline: Promotion generally follows the standard Army officer timeline (O-2→O-3 at ~2 years post-JAOBC, O-3→O-4 board eligibility at ~10 years), adjusted for the entry grade credit a JD/bar admission provides at commissioning. Not independently confirmed to primary-source level this session — treat as a general officer-promotion pattern, not a JAG-specific sourced figure.
reenlistment_decision_points: End of initial active-duty service obligation (varies by accession program — e.g., FLEP participants incur a longer service obligation in exchange for funded law school)
Assignment rotation among defense counsel, trial counsel (prosecution), legal assistance, and operational/administrative law billets
The 20-year retention decision (pension cliff — see retirement block)
advanced_schools_or_certs: Graduate Course at TJAGLCS (advanced legal education for career JAG officers, typically pursued mid-career) — not independently confirmed to primary-source level this session
LL.M in Military Law, awarded through TJAGLCS's Graduate Course — commonly reported, not independently confirmed this session

retirement.twenty_year_cliff_note: The pension is all-or-nothing at 20 years of COMMISSIONED (and any prior enlisted) service — no partial/prorated defined-benefit pension for separating early. Before 20 years, a Judge Advocate keeps every dollar of their own TSP contributions and earnings (always vested), plus DoD's automatic 1%-of-pay TSP contribution and match (vested after 2 years). Applies identically regardless of specialty; JAG carries no special retirement rule.
retirement.continuation_pay_note: One-time cash payout available between roughly year 7 and year 12 of service; Army's published active-component rate is 2.5x monthly basic pay for a 4-year additional service obligation (data/retirement.json). SEPARATELY, this session found that the JAG Corps offers its own distinct 'JAG Continuation Pay' incentive (per the Air Force search results this session) — the Army-specific amount and eligibility window for this JAG-specific incentive were NOT confirmed this session; do not conflate it with the general BRS continuation-pay figure above.
retirement.guard_reserve_points_note: Guard/Reserve retirement is NOT interchangeable with active-duty retirement. Points system toward a 20-'good year' qualifying threshold, non-regular pension generally starting at age 60. Applies to Army Reserve/National Guard JAG billets, which are a real and common path for attorneys who want to practice law as a civilian while serving part-time — not independently detailed this session.
retirement.lump_sum_option_note: At 20+ years, a retiring member may elect 25% or 50% of the discounted present value of future retired pay as an immediate lump sum, with correspondingly reduced monthly retired pay until Full Social Security Retirement Age (~67). Fully taxable in the year received.

transition.skillbridge_notes: DoD-wide SkillBridge eligibility is not specialty-restricted — subject to the same unit-commander approval gate as everyone else (data/transition.json). Law-firm SkillBridge placements for outgoing JAG officers were not independently verified this session.
transition.tap_program_summary: Mandatory DoD-wide Transition Assistance Program: pre-separation counseling, VA Benefits I course, DOL employment-fundamentals workshop, optional 2-day track, Capstone. Must begin no later than 365 days before separation. Applies identically regardless of specialty.
transition.dd214_significance: The DD-214's 'character of service' entry gates eligibility for nearly every benefit below. No JAG-specific significance beyond the general rule.
va_disability_common_claims: GUESS/INFERENCE, not sourced from a specialty-linked VA claims dataset this session (data/transition.json does not include a legal/JAG entry). Reasoned by analogy:

Mental-health conditions (PTSD, secondary traumatic stress, anxiety) plausibly elevated by direct, sustained exposure to court-martial case content involving sexual assault, child abuse, fatalities, and violent crime — a Judge Advocate's caseload exposure to this material is generally more direct and sustained than an enlisted paralegal's support role, making this a materially real occupational-mental-health consideration, though still a reasoned inference rather than a sourced VA claims-frequency figure
Musculoskeletal/repetitive-strain and vision-strain conditions from sustained desk/courtroom work, similar to the enlisted paralegal records

Deployed operational-law JAGs embedded with combat units may additionally carry the same theater/environmental-exposure risk (e.g., PACT Act-covered burn-pit exposure) as any other deployed service member in that theater — not JAG-specific, general deployment-location exposure
To verify: consult VA's published claims-by-occupation data or a VSO before treating this list as authoritative.
transition.vr_e_eligibility_note: Requires a 20%+ service-connected disability rating creating an employment handicap, filed within a 12-year window from separation or first rating. Funds up to 48 months of training/education plus a monthly subsistence allowance (FY2026 max ~$3,439.23/mo). No JAG-specific eligibility variance — though a licensed attorney with an existing JD/bar admission has less need for a full re-training benefit than most other veterans, VR&E can still fund additional bar admission/reciprocity costs or a different career pivot.

relevant_certifications: State bar admission in the state of intended civilian practice — a JAG's existing bar admission from commissioning does NOT automatically transfer to every other state; many states offer reduced-requirement or reciprocity admission for military attorneys, but this varies by state and is not universal
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: DIFFERENT KIND OF GAP than the enlisted records in this file: a JAG officer IS a licensed attorney (bar admission is a hard requirement to commission), so there is no missing base credential the way there is for enlisted paralegals. The real gap here is PORTABILITY, not existence: (1) state bar admission is jurisdiction-specific — a JAG barred in one state is not automatically barred in every state they might want to practice civilian law in after separation, though many states extend reciprocity or reduced-requirement admission to military attorneys (verify per-state before publishing, not sourced to a specific state list this session); (2) UCMJ/military-justice and operational/administrative-law experience, while substantial, does not automatically map to every civilian practice area (e.g., corporate transactional law, civil litigation procedure in a specific state's courts) without additional practice-area-specific experience or CLE.
civilian_crosswalk.source: BLS OEWS Lawyers (23-1011), May 2024 reference period — https://www.bls.gov/oes/2023/may/oes231011.htm (retrieved via web search synthesis this session; only the median figure was returned, not the full 10th/90th percentile table — the range above is a single median data point duplicated across both array positions, not a true low/high spread; treat as UNVERIFIED range pending a direct fetch of the BLS OEWS page).

veteran_benefits.gi_bill_tier_note: Post-9/11 GI Bill (Ch. 33) benefit tier scales with cumulative qualifying active-duty days: 100% at 36+ months, down through 90%, 80%, 70%, 60%, to a 50% floor at 90 days-5 months. No tier below 50%. Applies identically regardless of specialty or officer/enlisted status.
veteran_benefits.gi_bill_transferability_note: Transferable to a spouse and/or dependent children (up to 36 months combined) after 6 years of service, in exchange for a 4-year additional service-obligation commitment. Must be requested via milConnect while still serving.
veteran_benefits.va_home_loan_note: VA-backed home loans generally require no down payment and no monthly PMI for veterans with full entitlement, subject to lender underwriting and a funding fee (waived for veterans drawing service-connected disability compensation).
veteran_benefits.va_healthcare_priority_group_note: VA assigns one of 8 priority groups at enrollment based on disability rating, combat/toxic-exposure history, income, and other VA-benefit receipt — not by specialty or rank.
veteran_benefits.state_benefit_variance_note: State veteran benefits are set by individual state legislatures and are NOT a single national figure (data/veteran-benefits.json).

education_benefits.gi_bill_summary: Post-9/11 GI Bill pays full tuition/fees at public schools (in-state rate for all users) or up to $29,920.95/yr (2025-2026 AY) at private/foreign schools, plus zip-code-based housing allowance and a $1,000/yr book stipend. Most relevant here as a benefit a Judge Advocate could transfer to a dependent, since the JAG already holds a JD. (data/education-benefits.json)
education_benefits.tuition_assistance: DoD-wide Tuition Assistance ($250/semester-hour, $4,500/fiscal-year cap) exists but is of limited relevance to an officer who already holds a JD; more relevant is the Funded Legal Education Program (FLEP), which funds law school itself for competitively selected officers and enlisted members (confirmed open to enlisted this session — see the enlisted-track officer_conversion_path fields in this file).
education_benefits.credentialing_assistance: Not applicable in the traditional COOL sense — the JAG's credential (JD + bar admission) is a prerequisite for the job, not a credential pursued via Credentialing Assistance during service.
education_benefits.coop_or_apprenticeship_notes: The Funded Legal Education Program (FLEP) and the Educational Delay program (deferring active duty to attend law school after ROTC/Academy commissioning) are the two most relevant 'pre-JD' pathways into this track; the Graduate Law Program (GLP) and One-Year College Program (OYCP) are law-student-specific Air Force pathways sourced this session for the Air Force JAG record. Army-specific equivalents were referenced (jagcnet.army.mil) but not independently detailed this session.

deployment_and_lifestyle.typical_tempo: Judge Advocates deploy to provide operational law, military justice, and legal-assistance support to deployed commands; tempo follows the supported unit's deployment schedule rather than a JAG-specific rotation. No sourced deployment-frequency figure specific to Army JAG was found this session.
deployment_and_lifestyle.family_considerations: Garrison legal-office assignments (Staff Judge Advocate offices) are relatively family-stable; deployments follow the supported unit's schedule, which varies widely by assignment.

entry_requirements.asvab_line_score_note: Explicitly null, not an omission: the Air Force JAG Corps is a DIRECT COMMISSION path for licensed attorneys (or near-graduate law students, see below), not an enlistment path. There is no ASVAB line-score gate for this track. A 16-24-year-old reading this should understand: this is a post-law-school path, not something you enlist into with a high ASVAB score.
entry_requirements.education: A JD from an ABA-accredited law school, OR — for the Direct Appointment Program specifically — having completed at least two-thirds of the credit hours required for the JD, is required to apply. Attorneys applying after graduation must be graduates of an ABA-approved law school. (airforce.com/JAG FAQ, this session)
entry_requirements.age_range: UNVERIFIED this session — no Air Force-specific JAG age ceiling was located. Do NOT assume it is identical to the Army's confirmed under-42 ceiling without independent verification; check airforce.com/careers/specialty-careers/jag or jagusaf.jag.af.mil/appdocs/criteria.html before publishing.
entry_requirements.physical: Standard Air Force officer-commissioning medical and fitness standards apply; the 2026 officer-training restructuring (see training_pipeline) uses an abbreviated, non-line-officer track (OTS-A) that likely reflects a lighter physical standard than the line-officer OTS pipeline, but this was not independently confirmed this session.
entry_requirements.security_clearance: Not independently confirmed to a specific level this session. Reasoned inference: given exposure to classified operational-law and investigative matters, a Secret clearance at minimum (with Top Secret for some assignments) is plausible but was NOT sourced this session — verify before publishing.
entry_requirements.other: BINDING REQUIREMENT: admission to practice before any state's highest court or a federal court is required for attorney applicants — a JD alone does NOT qualify a candidate; passing a bar exam and being admitted is a separate, additional gate. Structurally distinct note: the Air Force JAG Corps also provides legal services to the SPACE FORCE — confirmed this session (airforce.com: 'The JAG Corps provides full-spectrum legal advice to the United States Air Force and Space Force'). This is the mechanism referenced in this file's branches_with_no_presence entry for Space Force: Space Force has no organic JAG Corps of its own and relies on this Air Force JAG Corps structure.
